topic: A person finds their childhood home listed for sale online
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on the screen, a stark, unwelcome intrusion into the mundane rhythm of my afternoon. It was an email, simple and direct: "Property at 412 Willow Creek Lane is now available for purchase." My fingers hovered over the screen, the cool glass a strange barrier between me and the reality flooding my chest. I reread the address, the faded yellow paint of the porch swing, the exact crooked angle of the oak tree in the front yard. A sudden, sharp pressure built behind my eyes, and I had to lean back in my chair, gripping the arms until my knuckles were white. I clicked the link, the ensuing photos—the peeling wallpaper, the familiar, slightly warped windowpane—felt less like images and more like invasive memories. A slow, cold dread began to seep into my bones, making my breath catch in my throat. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small living room, the silence of the house amplifying the frantic drumming of my own heart.

[story 2]

I found the listing tucked between listings for overpriced condos and dilapidated farmhouses. It was my house. The air seemed to thin out instantly. My hands started trembling, a fine, almost imperceptible vibration that I tried to mask by focusing intensely on the pattern of the rug beneath my feet. Every detail in the listing—the overgrown rose bushes, the specific shade of blue on the kitchen cabinets—hit me with the force of a physical blow. I walked to the window, pressing my forehead against the cool glass, trying to anchor myself to the present moment, but the past was clawing its way back in. A low, involuntary sound escaped my lips, a choked whisper of disbelief. I backed away from the window, stumbling slightly, my vision tunneling as the weight of this sudden, unwelcome reappearance crushed the air from my lungs.

[story 3]

The digital announcement landed with a sickening thud in my inbox. I stared at the screen, unable to move, my gaze fixed on the listing details as if they held the answer to a riddle I couldn't solve. A tightness constricted my chest, making each inhale shallow and ragged. I shuffled to the kitchen counter, my movements jerky and uncoordinated. I picked up a chipped ceramic mug, the familiar texture grounding me for a fleeting second, before setting it down with a clatter. The silence in the apartment suddenly felt cavernous, filled only with the frantic echo inside my own skull. I pressed my palms against my eyes, a desperate attempt to block out the visual evidence, but the image of that familiar front door, now framed by a "For Sale" sign, remained stubbornly etched behind my eyelids.
